topic: Area of Study 2: Popular Song - Blues, Jazz, Swing and Big Band
subject: music

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.q: What is a tone row in serial music?
items.correct: An ordered series of all 12 chromatic pitches, used as the basis of a piece.
items.misconception: A row of notes played together as a chord.
items.random_wrong: Any sequence of notes a composer chooses.
items.why: Students miss the SPECIFIC structure. Schoenberg's 12-tone technique uses ALL 12 pitches in a fixed order before any repeats, generating melodies and harmonies from that row.

items.correct: Rapid alternation between the written note and the note a step above.
items.why: Students confuse trill (rapid alternation between two notes) with vibrato (pitch oscillation around one note). Trills add ornamentation; vibrato adds expressive width to a sustained pitch.

items.q: Which Miles Davis album popularised modal jazz?
items.why: Correct answer: Kind of Blue. “Bitches Brew” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which saxophonist is most associated with the development of bebop?
items.why: It's Charlie Parker. “John Coltrane” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' released?
items.why: The real figure is 1959. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
